prompts.title: LLM Selection Decision Tree (Claude vs GPT vs Llama vs Gemini)
RAG vs Fine-Tune Decision Memo
Eval Metric Selection Framework
System Prompt Audit Checklist
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior prompt engineer. Audit the following system prompt and rewrite it.

Current system prompt:
```
{{paste current system prompt}}
```
Feature: {{what the agent does}}
Target model: {{Claude / GPT / Llama}}
Observed failures: {{list 3-5 failure modes from production logs}}

Deliver:
1. 12-point audit scorecard (1-5 each): role clarity, task specification, output format, edge-case handling, refusal rules, tone, length control, language handling (English/Hindi/Hinglish), grounding instructions, tool-use rules, safety guardrails, anti-jailbreak posture. Total /60.
2. For each score below 4, quote the exact offending line and explain what is wrong.
3. Rewritten system prompt using this structure: <role>, <context>, <task>, <constraints>, <output_format>, <examples>, <safety>. Use XML tags for Claude or markdown for GPT — pick based on target model.
4. Add 2 few-shot examples that cover the highest-frequency failure mode.
5. Add a self-check instruction (e.g., 'Before responding, verify your answer against constraints 1, 3, 5').
6. Token-count diff: old vs new (use tiktoken). If new prompt is > 30% longer, justify or shrink.
7. A/B test plan: traffic split, primary metric, sample size to reach 95% confidence, kill-switch.
8. Versioning advice: store in git with semver, log prompt-hash with every inference for traceability and Langfuse tracking.

Be direct. If the original prompt is unsalvageable, say so and start from scratch.

prompts.title: RAG Retriever Selection: Dense vs Sparse vs Hybrid
prompts.prompt:
Act as a RAG specialist. I am building retrieval over {{corpus type: Indian legal judgments / medical guidelines / Hindi fiction / product docs / customer tickets}} and need to choose between dense (e.g., bge-m3, e5-mistral, OpenAI text-embedding-3-large), sparse (BM25, SPLADE), and hybrid retrieval.

Corpus: {{number of docs, average doc length, languages, domain jargon density}}
Query patterns: {{short keyword / long natural-language / multi-hop}}
Update cadence: {{static / daily ingest / streaming}}
Latency SLO: {{ms}}
Budget: INR {{amount}}/month for embeddings + vector DB

Produce:
1. Recommendation: dense / sparse / hybrid with 1 line of reasoning.
2. Embedding model shortlist of 3, with comparison: dim, INR cost per 1M tokens, MIRACL/MTEB score, multilingual support (especially Hindi/Tamil/Bengali Indic-bench scores), context window.
3. Vector DB pick: Pinecone vs Weaviate vs Qdrant vs pgvector vs Milvus. Show INR cost at my scale and India-region availability for DPDP-Act compliance.
4. Chunking strategy specific to my corpus (legal: clause-aware, medical: section-aware, fiction: sliding window with overlap, docs: heading-aware). Specify chunk size, overlap, and metadata fields to attach.
5. Hybrid fusion method (RRF vs weighted) and re-ranker choice (Cohere Rerank India endpoint vs bge-reranker-v2 self-hosted).
6. Eval harness: 50-question seed set, recall@5, recall@20, MRR, faithfulness; how to evolve to 500 questions.
7. Index pseudocode (LangChain or LlamaIndex) showing ingest pipeline, metadata filter for multi-tenant isolation, and incremental upsert.
8. Cost-per-query in INR (embedding + retrieval + LLM call) and break-even analysis vs pure long-context Claude calls.
9. 3 failure modes specific to Indic text (tokenizer fragmentation, transliteration, code-mix) and mitigations.

prompts.title: Chunking Strategy for Indian Legal Corpus
prompts.prompt:
You are a RAG architect specialising in Indian legal tech. Design a chunking strategy for a corpus of {{Supreme Court judgments / High Court orders / Bare Acts / contracts}}.

Corpus stats: {{number of docs, avg pages, languages: English + Hindi + regional, citation density}}
Downstream task: {{semantic search / clause retrieval / citation extraction / Q&A for lawyers}}
Target LLM context: {{Claude 200k / GPT 128k / Llama 32k}}

Deliver:
1. Document-structure analysis: identify the natural units (judgment headnote, facts, issues, arguments, ratio, holding) and which units carry retrieval signal.
2. Chunking algorithm with pseudocode: hierarchical chunking (parent doc -> section -> paragraph -> sentence) with parent-child linking so retrieval returns small chunks but LLM sees parent context. Include token sizes (e.g., child 300 tok, parent 1500 tok).
3. Metadata schema: court, bench, judges, date, citation, party names, statutes-cited, sections-cited, language. Show how this enables filtered retrieval and citation graph building.
4. Handling cross-references: when a judgment cites another, store the citation as a graph edge in Neo4j or as metadata for second-hop retrieval.
5. Indic-language handling: detect language per chunk, route Hindi chunks through bge-m3 (multilingual) and English through bge-large-en, or use a single multilingual model with measured trade-offs.
6. Special tokens: preserve section symbols, footnote markers, paragraph numbering during chunking — these matter for legal citation.
7. Eval: 30-question lawyer-curated set, measure recall@10, citation-accuracy, ratio-vs-obiter discrimination.
8. DPDP-Act note: redact party names if corpus includes lower-court matters with personal data; build a redaction pipeline using Presidio + custom Indian-name recognisers.
9. Re-indexing strategy when new judgments arrive (incremental, no full rebuild).
10. INR cost estimate at 1M judgments: embedding (one-time + monthly delta), Qdrant Mumbai cluster, re-ranker calls.

Give me production-ready Python with LlamaIndex or LangChain.

prompts.title: Re-ranking Pipeline with Cost Trade-offs
prompts.prompt:
Design a re-ranking pipeline for my RAG system that improves recall@5 by at least 15% without breaking my latency or cost budget.

Baseline: {{describe current retriever, recall@5, p95 latency, INR cost per query}}
Query volume: {{queries/day}}
Budget: additional INR {{amount}}/month
Latency headroom: {{ms}}

Produce:
1. Re-ranker shortlist (3 options): Cohere Rerank 3 (API, INR per 1k searches), bge-reranker-v2-m3 (self-host on Modal A10), Jina Reranker (API). Compare quality (BEIR), latency (ms for 100 docs), and INR/month at my volume.
2. Pipeline design: retrieve top-50 with cheap dense, re-rank to top-5 with cross-encoder, then send to LLM. Show diagram and Python pseudocode.
3. When to skip re-ranking (cache hits, low-stakes queries) — use a query classifier (small Llama or distilbert) to route.
4. Latency budget breakdown: embed query (Xms) + ANN search (Yms) + re-rank (Zms) + LLM (Wms) = total. Where to parallelise.
5. Quality eval: A/B baseline vs re-ranked on 200-question set, measure recall@5, MRR, downstream answer-correctness.
6. Cost guardrail: alert when monthly re-rank spend crosses INR threshold; auto-fallback to retrieval-only if budget breached.
7. Multilingual gotcha: re-rankers trained on English may regress on Hindi/Tamil queries — measure separately and use a multilingual reranker if delta > 5%.
8. Caching: cache (query, doc_id) -> score pairs in Redis with 24h TTL; show hit-rate model.
9. Observability: log re-rank scores in Langfuse for offline analysis.
10. Rollout plan: 5% canary, watch for 48h, ramp.

prompts.title: RAG Eval Set Creation SOP
prompts.prompt:
You are an AI eval engineer. Help me build a high-quality RAG eval set from scratch for {{domain: Indian tax / e-commerce support / healthcare / education}}.

Corpus: {{describe}}
Users: {{persona}}
Launch deadline: {{date}}
Budget: INR {{amount}} for human labelling

Deliver step-by-step SOP:
1. Eval set sizing: 50 for prototype, 200 for launch, 1000 for steady-state. Justify with statistical power (binomial CI for accuracy estimation).
2. Question taxonomy: factual lookup (40%), multi-hop (25%), comparative (15%), summarisation (10%), out-of-scope refusal (10%). Adjust ratios for my domain.
3. Sourcing strategies (rank by quality): real user queries from logs (with consent + DPDP redaction), domain-expert hand-written, LLM-generated then human-validated, paraphrased from FAQs. Avoid pure-synthetic for launch.
4. Schema (JSON): question, expected_answer, gold_context_chunk_ids, difficulty (easy/med/hard), language (en/hi/ta/bn/hinglish), persona, must_refuse (bool), citations_required (bool).
5. Labelling protocol: 2-annotator agreement, third-arbiter for disagreements, inter-annotator kappa target > 0.7, payment INR per question.
6. Hindi/regional language coverage: at least 20% of questions in non-English to catch tokenizer + retrieval regressions.
7. Adversarial slice: 20 jailbreak prompts, 20 ambiguous queries, 20 questions whose answer is NOT in corpus (to test refusal).
8. Tooling: Argilla or Label Studio self-hosted on AWS Mumbai for DPDP-compliant annotation.
9. Metrics to compute per question: retrieval recall@5, faithfulness (Ragas), answer-correctness (LLM-judge with reasoning), citation-accuracy, latency, INR cost.
10. Versioning: tag eval set with semver, never delete questions, mark deprecated.
11. Refresh cadence: monthly add 50 new questions from production drift.

prompts.title: LoRA vs Full Fine-Tune Decision
Fine-Tuning Dataset Prep SOP
prompts.prompt:
I need to prepare a fine-tuning dataset for {{task}}. Walk me through the SOP from raw data to training-ready JSONL.

Raw data source: {{e.g., Zendesk tickets, internal docs, expert-curated Q&A, scraped public data}}
Volume: {{number of raw records}}
Target format: {{instruction-tuning / chat / DPO pairs}}
Base model template: {{Llama3 / Mistral / ChatML}}

Deliver step-by-step:
1. Data audit: count records, distribution by source/topic/language, quality sampling (50 random records reviewed manually with rubric).
2. Cleaning: dedup (MinHash + Jaccard 0.8), PII redaction with Presidio + Indian-name/Aadhaar/PAN/phone regex (DPDP-Act required), profanity filter, length filtering (drop < 20 tokens or > model context).
3. Decontamination: check overlap with eval set and known benchmarks (MMLU, IFEval) using n-gram match; remove leaked examples.
4. Augmentation: LLM-rewrite low-quality records, generate paraphrases for under-represented intents, translate Indic-only or English-only data to balance languages.
5. Format conversion: write a converter to chosen template; show ChatML and Llama3 examples side by side.
6. Quality scoring: use a judge LLM (Claude Haiku to keep cost low) to score each example on relevance, correctness, completeness; drop bottom 10%.
7. Stratified split: train/val/test by topic and language so val reflects production mix; never random-split if temporal data.
8. Train/eval contamination check post-split.
9. Metadata file: dataset card (HuggingFace format) with source, license, consent basis under DPDP-Act, intended use, known biases.
10. Versioning: store in S3 Mumbai with versioned prefix; track in DVC or LakeFS.
11. INR cost estimate: judge LLM calls + human review hours.
12. Final QA: sample 100 random rows from final JSONL, manually verify; ship only if > 95% pass.

prompts.title: Cost-per-1000-Token Calculator in INR
prompts.prompt:
Build me an INR cost-per-1000-token calculator for my product. I need to forecast monthly LLM bills and price features.

Features (give me a table I can fill):
- Feature name
- Model used (Claude Sonnet / GPT-4o / Gemini Flash / Llama-3-70B on Together)
- Avg input tokens per call
- Avg output tokens per call
- Calls per active user per month
- Active users

Deliver:
1. INR rate card (today's USD rates x 84 INR/USD, refresh quarterly): Claude 3.5 Sonnet input INR 0.25/1k, output INR 1.25/1k; Claude Haiku INR 0.07 / 0.34; GPT-4o INR 0.21 / 0.83; GPT-4o-mini INR 0.013 / 0.05; Gemini 1.5 Pro INR 0.10 / 0.42; Gemini Flash INR 0.006 / 0.025; Llama-3-70B on Together INR 0.07 blended; embedding-3-small INR 0.0017/1k.
2. Formula: monthly_inr = users * calls_per_user * (input_tok * input_rate + output_tok * output_rate) / 1000.
3. Spreadsheet schema (CSV header) ready to import into Google Sheets.
4. Sensitivity: show how cost changes if I swap model, add prompt-cache (90% hit rate cuts input cost 90%), or batch-API (50% discount).
5. Per-user gross margin: if I charge INR X/month for the feature, what is my LLM gross margin?
6. Alert thresholds: when monthly spend crosses 70% of forecast, page on-call.
7. Cost-attribution code snippet: log (user_id, feature, model, input_tok, output_tok, INR) per call to BigQuery / Postgres for analytics.

prompts.title: Prompt Caching Integration Plan (Anthropic / OpenAI)
prompts.prompt:
I am paying too much for repeated long context. Design a prompt-caching strategy using Anthropic prompt cache (5min / 1hr) or OpenAI prompt cache.

Feature: {{describe — e.g., RAG with same system prompt + tool defs, codebase chat with repo context, doc-analysis}}
Traffic: {{QPS, distinct users, repeat-query rate}}
Current INR cost / month: {{amount}}

Deliver:
1. Cacheability audit: which prompt segments are stable (system prompt, tool definitions, few-shot examples, large reference doc) vs dynamic (user query, retrieved chunks). Compute % of total tokens that are stable.
2. Cache-segment design with code: place cache_control breakpoints at the boundary between stable and dynamic. Show Anthropic SDK example with 4 breakpoints (system, tools, retrieved-corpus, conversation history).
3. Hit-rate model: estimate cache hit rate from traffic pattern; conservative 60%, optimistic 85%.
4. INR savings calc: cached read is 0.1x cost; show monthly savings = cached_input_tokens * 0.9 * input_rate.
5. TTL strategy: 5-min for hot conversations, 1-hour for shared corpus across users; cost trade-off (1-hour write is 2x).
6. Multi-tenant gotcha: if cache key includes user-specific data, hit rate collapses; restructure prompt to put shared content first.
7. Observability: log cache_creation_input_tokens and cache_read_input_tokens; build dashboard showing hit rate over time.
8. Edge cases: cache invalidation when corpus updates, cold-start cost, eviction.
9. Rollout: enable on 10% traffic, measure savings + latency (cache hits are also faster), then 100%.
10. ROI: payback period in days.

prompts.title: Streaming SSE Design for LLM Responses
prompts.prompt:
Design a server-sent-events streaming layer for our LLM product. Backend: {{Node / Python FastAPI / Go}}. Frontend: {{Next.js / React Native}}. Provider: {{OpenAI / Anthropic / Together vLLM}}.

Requirements: low TTFT, graceful disconnect, retry on transient errors, render markdown/code progressively, support tool-use mid-stream, support cancellation, log to Langfuse.

Deliver:
1. Architecture diagram: client -> edge (Cloudflare / CloudFront Mumbai) -> API gateway -> LLM proxy (LiteLLM) -> provider. Note where streaming buffers must be disabled.
2. SSE event schema: event types (token, tool_call, error, usage, done) with JSON payloads. Show TypeScript interfaces.
3. Backend code skeleton: FastAPI generator that yields SSE-formatted strings; handle provider stream parsing for both Anthropic and OpenAI deltas.
4. Cancellation: client closes EventSource -> server detects disconnect -> aborts upstream LLM call to save INR.
5. Retry on mid-stream failure: keep accumulated text, retry from where it stopped using continuation prompt or restart with idempotency key.
6. Backpressure: if client lags, buffer up to N tokens then drop or close.
7. Multi-region: serve from AWS Mumbai for India users to minimise RTT; document residency for DPDP compliance.
8. Frontend: useEventSource hook with reconnect, markdown-streaming renderer that handles partial code blocks, typing indicator.
9. Observability: emit (request_id, ttft_ms, total_ms, tokens, INR cost, cache_hit) to Langfuse on stream end.
10. Load test plan: k6 with 500 concurrent streams, measure p95 TTFT and disconnect rate.

prompts.title: Rate-Limit and Retry Strategy
prompts.prompt:
Design rate-limit handling and retry logic for my LLM client that hits {{Anthropic / OpenAI / Bedrock / Vertex / Together}}.

Traffic: {{peak QPS}}
Provider quotas: {{TPM, RPM, ITPM}}
Latency SLO: {{ms p95}}
Failure budget: {{% per month}}

Deliver:
1. Quota inventory table: per-provider RPM, TPM, output-TPM, organisation-wide vs key-wide. Show current utilisation.
2. Client-side rate limiter (token-bucket) with code in Python (asyncio + aiolimiter) or Node (bottleneck). Refill rate set to 80% of provider limit for safety.
3. Retry policy: retry on 429, 502, 503, 529; exponential backoff with jitter (base 1s, cap 32s, max 5 attempts); read Retry-After header when present.
4. Idempotency: pass anthropic-idempotency-key header to avoid double-billing on retry.
5. Budget per request: max 30s wall-clock; if exceeded, fail-fast and return cached / smaller-model response.
6. Multi-provider failover: primary Claude -> fallback GPT-4o -> fallback Llama-3-70B Together. Use LiteLLM router with health checks.
7. Per-tenant quota: prevent one customer from exhausting org quota; sliding-window in Redis.
8. Queue spillover: if rate-limited, push to SQS / RabbitMQ for async processing; user gets webhook callback.
9. Observability: emit (provider, status_code, retries, latency, queue_wait) metrics to Prometheus / CloudWatch India.
10. Chaos test: weekly fault-injection that throttles primary provider; measure failover correctness and INR cost shift.
11. Runbook for the on-call when provider has a regional outage.

prompts.title: Observability with Langfuse / Helicone
prompts.prompt:
Set up LLM observability for our production stack. Choose between Langfuse (self-host on AWS Mumbai for DPDP) and Helicone (US-hosted SaaS).

Stack: {{LangChain / LlamaIndex / vanilla SDK}}, {{Anthropic / OpenAI}}, FastAPI backend, Postgres.

Deliver:
1. Vendor pick: Langfuse self-host vs Helicone. Compare on DPDP-Act fit (data residency in India), cost (INR/month at 10M traces), feature set (tracing, evals, prompt management, datasets), and integration effort.
2. Trace schema: trace -> spans -> generations. Required attributes (user_id, session_id, feature, model, prompt_version, input_tokens, output_tokens, INR_cost, latency_ms, cache_hit, retrieval_chunks, tool_calls, error).
3. Instrumentation: code snippets to wrap Anthropic / OpenAI clients with Langfuse decorators or proxy via Helicone.
4. PII handling: hash user_id with HMAC, redact prompts containing Aadhaar/PAN/phone before sending to observability backend (use Presidio).
5. Sampling: 100% trace for new features, 10% for stable, 100% for errors and high-cost traces.
6. Dashboards: cost by feature INR/day, p95 latency by model, error rate by tenant, hallucination rate from LLM-judge runs, prompt-version A/B comparison.
7. Alerts: cost spike > 30% week-over-week, error rate > 2%, latency p95 > SLO, refusal rate spike.
8. Eval-on-traces: weekly job that pulls 200 random production traces and runs LLM-judge for faithfulness; track drift.
9. Retention: 90 days hot, archive to S3 Glacier; respect DPDP user-deletion requests.
10. Rollout plan with team adoption.

prompts.title: Vector DB Selection: Pinecone vs Weaviate vs Qdrant vs pgvector
prompts.prompt:
Pick a vector DB for my product.

Use case: {{describe}}
Vectors: {{count, dim, expected growth}}
QPS: {{number, peak}}
Filters: {{multi-tenant, metadata, hybrid keyword}}
Latency SLO: {{p95 ms}}
Budget: INR {{amount}}/month
Data residency: {{India required / global ok}}

Deliver:
1. Shortlist: Pinecone Serverless, Weaviate Cloud, Qdrant Cloud, Milvus (Zilliz), pgvector on RDS Mumbai. Eliminate any that fail residency or budget upfront.
2. Comparison table: managed regions (esp Mumbai/Hyderabad), pricing model (storage + queries), HNSW vs IVF vs DiskANN, hybrid-search support, multi-tenant model (namespace vs collection vs filter), backup/restore, SLA.
3. INR/month at my scale for top 2 candidates.
4. Index config: M, ef_construction, ef_search; trade-off recall vs latency vs INR.
5. Multi-tenant strategy: namespace-per-tenant (best isolation, higher cost) vs single-collection-with-metadata-filter (cheap but pre-filter perf risk). Pick and justify.
6. Hybrid search: how to combine BM25 + dense; native support vs application-layer RRF.
7. Sharding plan when vectors cross 50M.
8. Embedding-dim trade-off: dim 1536 vs 768 vs 384 — show INR storage diff and recall delta on my domain.
9. Migration plan if I outgrow pgvector -> Qdrant: dual-write, backfill, cutover.
10. Disaster recovery: snapshot frequency, RPO/RTO, cross-region replica.
11. Final recommendation in 3 lines + 1 risk + cost.
12. Proof-of-concept code (Python) that ingests 10k vectors, runs filtered hybrid search, and measures recall@10 and latency.

prompts.title: Embedding Model Trade-off Analysis
prompts.prompt:
I need to pick an embedding model. Compare candidates for my domain.

Domain: {{e.g., Hindi news, English customer support, code, legal, multilingual e-commerce search}}
Volume: {{1M chunks at launch, 10M in 1 year}}
Query latency: {{ms}}

Candidates to evaluate: text-embedding-3-large (3072 / 1536), text-embedding-3-small, bge-m3 (multilingual, 1024), bge-large-en (1024), e5-mistral-7b-instruct (4096), Cohere embed-multilingual-v3 (1024), Jina v3 (1024), nomic-embed-text-v1.5, voyage-3.

Deliver:
1. Evaluation rubric: MTEB score for my language(s), MIRACL for multilingual, dim, max sequence length, INR cost per 1M tokens (API) or INR per A10/A100-hour (self-host on Modal), licence.
2. Self-host vs API decision: at my volume, when does self-host on Modal become cheaper? Show the break-even token count.
3. Indic-specific: bge-m3 and multilingual-e5 dominate for Hindi/Tamil/Bengali; show MIRACL-Hi scores side by side.
4. Dim-reduction: text-embedding-3-large supports MRL truncation to 512 dim; show storage INR savings and recall impact on my eval set.
5. Recommendation: 1 primary + 1 fallback. Justify in 2 lines.
6. Migration plan: re-embed 10M chunks; cost in INR + GPU-hours; dual-index window for canary.
7. Eval harness: 100-pair semantic-similarity test in my domain; measure spearman correlation between model and gold scores.
8. Caching: cache (text -> vector) in Redis to avoid re-embedding identical chunks during ingest retries; INR savings.

prompts.title: Multi-Tenant Vector DB Filtering Strategy
prompts.prompt:
Design multi-tenant isolation for my vector DB. We have {{number}} tenants, each with {{avg vectors}} and strict data isolation under DPDP-Act.

Vector DB: {{Pinecone / Qdrant / Weaviate / pgvector}}.

Deliver:
1. Isolation-model decision tree: namespace-per-tenant (Pinecone), collection-per-tenant (Qdrant/Weaviate), shared-collection-with-tenant_id-filter, separate-cluster-per-enterprise-customer. Show pros (isolation, perf, blast radius) and cons (cost, ops overhead) for each.
2. Recommendation based on tenant size distribution (long-tail of small tenants + a few enterprise) — typically: shared collection + tenant_id filter for SMB, dedicated namespace for enterprise.
3. Filter performance: pre-filter vs post-filter — for shared-collection, set up payload index on tenant_id (Qdrant) or namespace (Pinecone) to keep recall.
4. Auth boundary: tenant_id is enforced at API gateway from JWT; never trust client-supplied tenant_id.
5. Cross-tenant leak prevention: integration test that ingests data for tenant A, queries with tenant B's token, asserts zero results. Run in CI.
6. Cost model in INR: per-tenant storage and query cost, billing pass-through.
7. Tenant deletion (DPDP right-to-erasure): API to delete all vectors by tenant_id within 30 days; verify with a sentinel query post-delete.
8. Backup per tenant for enterprise tier; encryption keys per tenant if regulator demands.
9. Monitoring: per-tenant QPS, recall, latency.
10. Migration: move enterprise tenant from shared to dedicated namespace without downtime.

prompts.title: Agent Architecture: Planning vs ReAct
prompts.prompt:
Design an agent for {{task: e.g., automate Razorpay refund flow / triage customer email / book a meeting / extract data from invoices / browse the web for research}}.

Tools available: {{list}}
Max steps: {{number}}
Latency budget: {{seconds}}
Cost budget per task: INR {{amount}}
Failure cost: {{low / med / high}}

Produce:
1. Pattern decision: ReAct (interleaved thought + action) vs plan-and-execute (LLM writes full plan then executes) vs reflexion (try -> critique -> retry) vs LLM-compiler (parallel tool calls). Justify pick in 3 lines.
2. State machine diagram (Mermaid): nodes for plan, act, observe, verify, finalise; edges for retry, escalate, fail.
3. System prompt scaffold with role, available tools (with strict JSON schemas), decision rules, and stop conditions.
4. Tool schemas (OpenAPI-like JSON) including pre-conditions and post-conditions; example: refund_payment(payment_id, reason) with idempotency_key required.
5. Safety wrappers: human-in-loop for any action above INR 1000, dry-run mode, reversible vs irreversible action classification.
6. Memory: short-term scratchpad in prompt vs long-term in vector DB; what to persist across sessions.
7. Loop guard: max 8 steps, detect cycle (same tool with same args twice), token budget cap.
8. Eval harness: 30 task scenarios with expected tool sequence + final state; measure success rate, avg steps, INR cost, latency.
9. Observability: log every tool call with input/output, agent_trace_id; render in Langfuse.
10. Model pick: Claude Sonnet for planning, Haiku for tool-call routing; show INR/task and quality.
11. Rollout: shadow mode for 1 week, then 5% live, then 100%.

prompts.title: Tool-Use Safety Review Checklist
prompts.prompt:
Audit our agent's tool-use for safety. Tools: {{list with descriptions, e.g., send_email, create_invoice, query_db, run_python, browse_web, refund_payment}}.

Deliver:
1. Per-tool risk rating (1-5): reversibility, blast radius, financial impact, data exposure, regulatory risk (DPDP, RBI).
2. Identify any tool that should NEVER be auto-executed without human approval (typically anything irreversible or > INR 1000 financial impact).
3. Schema audit: for each tool, ensure input is strictly typed (JSON schema), enums are closed, free-text fields are length-capped, IDs are validated against an allowlist.
4. Pre-flight checks: idempotency key, dry-run flag, rate-limit per tool per user, sanity bounds (e.g., refund_amount <= original_payment).
5. Prompt-injection defence: when tool output (e.g., scraped web page, DB row) is fed back to the model, sandwich it with delimiters and instruct the model to treat it as untrusted data.
6. PII / DPDP: tools that read PII must log access; tools that write PII must respect consent.
7. Audit log: every tool call recorded with (agent_id, user_id, tool, args_hash, result_hash, timestamp, approver if any) — retain 1 year for SEBI/RBI-regulated flows.
8. Kill switch: per-tool feature flag to disable without redeploy.
9. Red-team: 20 adversarial prompts that try to make the agent misuse each tool; document outcomes.
10. Sign-off matrix: which roles approve which tools to go live.

prompts.title: Multi-Agent Orchestration Design
prompts.prompt:
Design a multi-agent system for {{complex workflow: e.g., end-to-end loan processing, multi-step research, customer-onboarding KYC + risk + funding, data-pipeline build}}.

Sub-agent candidates: {{list, e.g., document-extractor, KYC-checker, risk-scorer, decision-maker, customer-comms}}.

Deliver:
1. Topology pick: supervisor (one orchestrator routes to specialists), graph (LangGraph DAG), swarm (peer-to-peer hand-off), hierarchical (manager + workers + sub-workers). Justify.
2. Agent inventory: name, model (Claude Sonnet for reasoning, Haiku for routing, Llama-3-70B for high-volume extraction), tools, system prompt focus, INR cost per invocation.
3. State schema (TypedDict / pydantic): shared state passed between agents — fields, owners, immutability rules.
4. Hand-off protocol: structured message format (sender, receiver, intent, payload, confidence); avoid free-text passing.
5. Concurrency: which agents can run in parallel (e.g., KYC + risk-score) vs serial dependencies; show LangGraph diagram.
6. Error recovery: per-agent retry policy, fallback agent, escalate-to-human node.
7. Cycle prevention: max-hops per task, supervisor sees full history, dedup on identical state.
8. Cost guardrail: per-task INR budget; abort if budget breached and emit alert.
9. Eval harness: 20 end-to-end scenarios with expected agent path and final outcome; measure task success, agent-call count, total INR.
10. Observability: trace tree per task with each agent's prompt/response, tool calls, latency, INR.
11. Failure mode analysis: 5 ways the system can silently produce a wrong outcome (e.g., extractor hallucinates, decision-maker sees stale state) and mitigations.
12. Rollout: shadow for 2 weeks, compare against human baseline.

prompts.title: Agent Error-Recovery Loop Design
prompts.prompt:
My agent fails silently or loops infinitely on edge cases. Design a robust error-recovery loop.

Current failure modes: {{list 5 with frequencies from production logs}}.
Agent stack: {{LangGraph / CrewAI / custom}}.

Deliver:
1. Error taxonomy: tool-error (4xx/5xx), schema-error (LLM returned bad JSON), planning-error (no valid next step), policy-error (refusal), budget-error (token/INR cap), timeout, hallucination.
2. Per-class handler with code snippet:
   - tool-error: retry with backoff up to N, fallback tool, ask user for missing info, escalate.
   - schema-error: re-prompt with the validation error message; switch to JSON-mode or structured output (Anthropic tool-use, OpenAI strict mode).
   - planning-error: reflexion step where LLM critiques its plan and replans once.
   - hallucination: verifier sub-agent re-checks claim against source; on mismatch, retry with stricter grounding instruction.
3. Loop guard: max 8 iterations, detect repeated state hash, cost ceiling INR per task.
4. Graceful degradation: if everything fails, return a partial result with a transparent 'I could not complete X because Y' message, never silent failure.
5. Replay: persist full state to Postgres so a failed task can be resumed manually.
6. Alerting: error-class counts to Datadog / Grafana; page on-call when class crosses threshold.
7. Eval: build 30 adversarial cases that trigger each error class; ensure recovery success rate > 80%.
8. Post-mortem template for each new class observed in prod.

prompts.title: Offline Eval Framework Setup
prompts.prompt:
Bootstrap an offline-eval framework for our LLM product so every PR runs evals in CI.

Stack: {{Python, GitHub Actions, Langfuse / Braintrust / promptfoo}}.

Deliver:
1. Tooling pick: promptfoo (yaml-driven, cheap), DeepEval (pytest-style, rich metrics), Braintrust (hosted UI), Langfuse Datasets (self-host). Choose based on team workflow + DPDP residency.
2. Folder layout:
   evals/
     datasets/  (jsonl golden sets, versioned)
     metrics/   (custom scorers)
     prompts/   (prompt templates with semver)
     run.py     (entry point)
     ci.yml     (GH Actions workflow)
3. Dataset schema (jsonl): id, input, expected_output, expected_context, language, persona, difficulty, must_refuse, metadata.
4. Metric implementations: exact_match, f1, ragas_faithfulness, ragas_answer_correctness, llm_judge_with_reasoning (template included), latency_ms, INR_cost, refusal_rate, format_adherence.
5. CI workflow: on PR, run eval on 100-question subset; on main merge, run full 500; gate merge if primary metric drops > 3%.
6. Cost control: parallelise with semaphore = 10; cap INR per CI run at 200; cache identical (prompt, model) responses.
7. Reporting: post a comment on PR with table of metric deltas vs baseline; link to full Langfuse trace.
8. Versioning: every eval run tagged with (prompt_version, model, dataset_version, code_sha) so results are reproducible.
9. Human review queue: bottom-quartile outputs go to Argilla for weekly human review; findings feed new test cases.
10. Onboarding doc: 1-pager 'how to add a new test case in 5 minutes' so PMs and domain experts can contribute.

prompts.title: LLM-as-Judge with Reasoning
prompts.prompt:
Build an LLM-as-judge scorer for {{metric: faithfulness / answer-correctness / helpfulness / harmfulness / tone-match}}.

Judge model: {{Claude Sonnet / GPT-4o}}. Subject model: {{any}}.

Deliver:
1. Judge prompt template using XML for Claude or markdown for GPT: <task>, <criteria_definition>, <input>, <reference>, <candidate>, <instructions: think step-by-step, then output JSON {reasoning, score 1-5, label}>. Force reasoning before score to reduce judge bias.
2. Rubric: precise definitions for score 1-5 with example for each level so judges are consistent across runs.
3. Calibration: 50 human-labelled examples; run judge, compute Cohen kappa or weighted agreement; iterate prompt until kappa > 0.6.
4. Bias mitigation: position-swap when comparing two candidates (avoid first-listed preference), strip names/IDs, randomise order.
5. Multi-judge ensemble: run 3 different judges (Claude, GPT, Gemini) and majority-vote for high-stakes metrics; cost trade-off in INR.
6. Cost: estimate INR per 1000 judgements; use Claude Haiku for cheap pre-screen and Sonnet for borderline cases (cascade pattern).
7. Self-consistency: run judge 3 times at temp 0.3, take median.
8. Output schema: {reasoning: str, score: int, label: enum, confidence: float}; validate with pydantic.
9. Failure detection: if judge refuses or returns malformed JSON, retry with more constrained prompt; log incidents.
10. Drift monitoring: re-run calibration set monthly; alert if judge agreement drops > 5%.
11. When NOT to use LLM-judge (deterministic tasks like SQL execution, math).

prompts.title: Regression Test Set for Prompts
prompts.prompt:
I keep regressing prompts when I change them. Build a regression test set for my prompt library.

Prompts in scope: {{list}}
Versioning: {{git / Langfuse Prompts / Braintrust Prompts}}.

Deliver:
1. Per-prompt golden set of 30 examples sourced from: production logs (anonymised), past bug reports, user-feedback negatives, hand-curated edge cases (Hindi/Hinglish, long input, ambiguous, must-refuse).
2. Metric set per prompt: 1 primary (e.g., answer-correctness), 2 guardrails (refusal-rate, format-adherence).
3. Baseline run: store metric scores for current prompt-version as the reference; commit as JSON to repo.
4. Diff workflow: when a PR changes a prompt, CI re-runs the golden set with the new prompt; produces a diff table; fails if primary drops > X% or any guardrail breached.
5. Few-shot drift check: snapshot the LLM responses themselves (not just metrics); if response embedding drifts > threshold from baseline, flag for review even if metric is stable.
6. Prompt-version pinning: every production call logs prompt_version_hash; rollback by re-pointing version pointer.
7. Eval cost: cap INR per CI run; use cheaper model for fast feedback + Sonnet for nightly.
8. New-case capture: a 'flag this output' button in product creates a Jira ticket and pre-fills a regression-test stub.
9. Quarterly audit: prune stale cases, refresh from production drift.

prompts.title: Human-Eval Rubric for Hallucination
prompts.prompt:
Design a human-eval rubric for hallucination in my RAG product. Will be used by 5 reviewers, INR 80 per question.

Domain: {{e.g., Indian tax advisory, medical Q&A, customer support}}.

Deliver:
1. Hallucination taxonomy:
   - intrinsic (contradicts retrieved context),
   - extrinsic (adds unsupported claims not in context),
   - factual (contradicts ground-truth knowledge),
   - citation-fabrication (cites non-existent or wrong source),
   - language-mix error (wrong Hindi/English term).
2. Rubric (5-point per dimension): 1 = severe, 5 = none. Provide an example response at each level for each dimension.
3. Reviewer task UI: show question, retrieved chunks (highlighted), candidate answer, dimension sliders, free-text justification box, and a 'show citations' toggle.
4. Inter-rater reliability: 50 shared questions across all 5 reviewers; compute Krippendorff alpha target > 0.7; calibration session before live labelling.
5. Sampling strategy: stratify by feature, language, query difficulty; over-sample known-failing slices.
6. Throughput: target 30 questions / reviewer / hour; total INR for 1000 questions.
7. Tooling: Argilla self-hosted on Mumbai EC2 for DPDP residency; export to JSONL for ML team.
8. Adjudication: any item with std-dev > 1 across reviewers escalated to senior reviewer.
9. Output: hallucination rate by dimension by feature, dashboard in Metabase.
10. Loop back: failing examples become regression tests.

prompts.title: Few-Shot vs Zero-Shot Decision
prompts.prompt:
Help me decide whether to use few-shot examples in my prompt for {{task}}.

Current state: {{zero-shot baseline metric, prompt length, model, INR cost per call}}.

Deliver:
1. Decision rule: prefer zero-shot when (a) task is well-known to the model, (b) context window is tight, (c) per-call cost is sensitive. Prefer few-shot when (a) format is unusual, (b) edge cases are common, (c) accuracy gap > 5% closes with examples.
2. Few-shot scaling experiment: test 0, 2, 4, 8 shots on the eval set; plot accuracy vs INR cost vs latency.
3. Example selection: random vs nearest-neighbour (retrieve k examples per query from a curated bank using embeddings) vs hand-picked diverse set. NN is usually best for QA, hand-picked for format-locked tasks.
4. Example bank curation: 50-200 high-quality (input, output) pairs; refresh quarterly.
5. Token budget: shots cost input tokens; with prompt caching they amortise cheaply — combine few-shot + Anthropic prompt cache for 90% input-cost reduction.
6. Anti-pattern: never include a shot whose output style you would NOT accept from production.
7. Eval before/after: report accuracy lift, INR delta, latency delta.
8. Final pick + justification.

prompts.title: Structured Output: XML vs JSON Pattern
prompts.prompt:
I need structured output from {{Claude / GPT / Llama}}. Recommend whether to use XML, JSON, or native tool-use / structured-output mode.

Use case: {{describe — e.g., extract entities from invoice, classify ticket, write multi-section report}}.
Downstream consumer: {{Python pydantic / TypeScript zod / SQL insert}}.

Deliver:
1. Mode decision:
   - Claude: prefer XML tags for legibility OR tool-use for strict schema.
   - OpenAI: use Structured Outputs (response_format: json_schema) with strict: true — guarantees valid JSON matching schema.
   - Llama / open models: JSON mode + outlines/instructor library for grammar-constrained decoding.
2. Schema design: keep < 10 top-level fields, use enums where possible, mark optional fields, version the schema.
3. Pydantic / Zod model with example.
4. Failure handling: if parsing fails, retry once with the validation error appended; second failure -> log + return safe default.
5. Token efficiency: JSON quotes and braces add ~20% tokens vs XML; for huge outputs prefer XML with delimited sections.
6. Streaming: structured outputs stream as partial JSON — use a tolerant parser (e.g., partial-json-parser).
7. Guard against prompt injection that breaks the schema (e.g., user input containing closing tags).
8. Eval: format-adherence rate target > 99.5%.
9. Migration plan if you outgrow regex-extraction to native structured mode.

prompts.title: Chain-of-Thought Safety Review
prompts.prompt:
Audit our use of chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting and recommend a safer pattern.

Features using CoT: {{list}}.
Observed issues: {{e.g., reasoning leaks PII, reasoning disagrees with final answer, latency too high}}.

Deliver:
1. Risks of exposing CoT: (a) leaks internal logic / IP, (b) leaks data the model retrieved, (c) doubles output cost in INR, (d) increases latency, (e) reasoning can be jailbroken to surface unsafe content.
2. Pattern recommendations:
   - Hidden CoT: ask model to reason inside <thinking> tags then strip before showing user.
   - Extended thinking (Claude): use thinking budget; never log raw thinking to user-visible places; redact PII from thinking before storage.
   - Self-consistency: 3 reasoning paths, majority-vote final answer; use Haiku to keep INR low.
   - Skip CoT for simple classification — it adds cost without quality lift.
3. Logging: store reasoning in a separate table with stricter access controls; mask PII before persisting.
4. Latency budget: with thinking, p95 can 2-3x; pre-warm and stream thinking to internal observability only.
5. Eval: measure quality with vs without CoT on golden set; only keep CoT where lift > 5%.
6. DPDP note: reasoning that contains user PII is in scope; treat as personal data.

prompts.title: Vision LLM for Aadhaar OCR
prompts.prompt:
Design an Aadhaar OCR pipeline using a vision LLM. Strict DPDP-Act and UIDAI compliance required.

Volume: {{docs/day}}
Latency SLO: {{seconds}}
Device mix: {{mobile uploads, scans, photos with glare}}

Deliver:
1. Compliance gate first: confirm we have lawful basis (consent + KYC purpose), Aadhaar number must be masked except last 4 digits in any storage / log per UIDAI guidelines, never persist raw image beyond 24h, encrypt at rest with KMS.
2. Vision model pick: Claude 3.5 Sonnet vision, GPT-4o vision, Gemini 1.5 Flash vision, open-source Qwen2-VL-7B self-hosted on Modal A10. Compare INR/image, accuracy on noisy Indian IDs, hallucination rate.
3. Pipeline: pre-process (deskew, denoise, mask QR if present) -> vision LLM extract -> post-process (regex validate Aadhaar 12-digit, name pattern, DOB) -> Verhoeff-checksum on Aadhaar number -> face-match (separate service) -> store masked record.
4. Prompt design: structured-output JSON schema {name, dob, gender, address, last4, doc_type, confidence}; instruct model to return null for unreadable fields rather than guess.
5. Hallucination guard: 2-pass extract with different prompts; flag mismatches for human review.
6. Indic name handling: model must preserve Devanagari/Tamil/Bengali script if present; do not transliterate without flag.
7. Eval set: 500 real Aadhaar samples (with consent, then deleted) covering 10 states, 5 doc-quality bands; measure field-level accuracy.
8. Cost in INR per doc.
9. Audit log: who accessed which record, retained 1 year, encrypted.
10. Fallback: if confidence < threshold, route to human ops queue.
11. Red-team: synthetic forged Aadhaars to test refusal.

prompts.title: Voice LLM for IVR in Hindi
prompts.prompt:
Design a voice agent for our IVR that speaks Hindi (and code-mix Hinglish). Use case: {{e.g., loan-EMI status, food-delivery order, telecom recharge, doctor-appointment booking}}.

Volume: {{calls/day}}
Latency target: {{ms response after user pause}}
Languages: Hindi primary, English fallback, Hinglish acceptable.

Deliver:
1. Stack pick: STT (Sarvam STT, Bhashini, Deepgram, OpenAI Whisper-large-v3), LLM (Claude Sonnet, GPT-4o, Llama-3-70B on Together), TTS (Sarvam TTS, ElevenLabs Hindi, Bhashini, OpenAI). Compare WER on Hindi accents, MOS, INR/min, India hosting.
2. Architecture: telephony (Exotel / Knowlarity / Twilio) -> STT streaming -> LLM with low-latency prompt -> TTS streaming -> back to caller. Show p95 budget allocation across each hop.
3. Barge-in handling: detect user speech mid-bot-utterance, cut TTS, restart STT.
4. Prompt design: short system prompt in Hindi script with English fallback rule; tools for backend lookups (account status, order status); strict refusal on out-of-scope.
5. Code-switching: instruct LLM to mirror user language register (formal Hindi vs Hinglish).
6. Latency tricks: stream LLM tokens to TTS sentence-by-sentence; use Haiku/Flash for routing, Sonnet for complex turns; pre-warm caches.
7. INR cost per minute: STT + LLM tokens + TTS chars; benchmark < INR 4 / min.
8. Number / OTP capture: confirm digit-by-digit, use NATO-equivalent in Hindi for clarity.
9. Compliance: TRAI consent for recording, DPDP for storing call audio, deletion after 90 days unless dispute.
10. Eval: 100 sample call scripts across intents and accents (Bihari, Punjabi, South-Indian Hindi); measure task-success, AHT, escalation rate.
11. Fallback to human agent on 2nd misunderstanding.

prompts.title: Code LLM for IDE Assistant
prompts.prompt:
Build an in-IDE code assistant for our internal monorepo. Languages: {{TypeScript, Python, Go}}. Repo size: {{LOC}}. Team: {{n}} developers.

Deliver:
1. Model pick: Claude Sonnet (best code reasoning), GPT-4o, DeepSeek-Coder-V2, Codestral, Llama-3.1-405B. Compare HumanEval, SWE-bench, repo-level QA, INR per dev per month.
2. Context strategy: open-file + cursor + recently-edited (FIM); tree-sitter symbol graph for cross-file references; embed entire repo with bge-code or jina-code, retrieve top-k chunks per query.
3. Repo indexing: incremental on git push, store in Qdrant Mumbai; metadata = (path, symbol, language, last_modified).
4. Prompt scaffold: <system: senior engineer in our stack>, <repo_context: retrieved chunks>, <open_file>, <cursor_diff>, <user_query>; structured output for diff suggestions.
5. Inline-completion vs chat: low-latency FIM (small fast model like Codestral or DeepSeek-Coder-1.3B) for autocomplete; large model for multi-file refactor in chat.
6. Privacy: route through internal proxy on AWS Mumbai; never send code to vendor outside India for clients with sovereignty clauses; offer self-host (Llama on vLLM) tier.
7. Eval: 50 real PRs replayed; measure (a) bug-introduction rate, (b) test-pass rate of suggested code, (c) acceptance rate by devs.
8. Cost: INR per dev per month; cache stable repo context with Anthropic prompt cache.
9. Guardrails: never auto-commit; require human approval for any change touching auth/payments.
10. Telemetry: opt-in, anonymised, used to fine-tune ranking model.

prompts.title: Document Extraction for ITR Filing
prompts.prompt:
Build an LLM-powered document-extraction pipeline for Indian ITR filing. Input docs: Form-16, Form-26AS, AIS, bank statements, broker P&L, rent receipts.

Volume: {{docs/season}}
Accuracy bar: > 99% on amount fields
Latency: batch ok, < 30s/doc preferred.

Deliver:
1. Doc-classifier first: small vision LLM (Gemini Flash or Qwen2-VL) routes each upload to the right extractor.
2. Per-doc extractor: vision LLM with doc-specific JSON schema (e.g., Form-16: gross_salary, sec_10_exemptions, tds_deducted, employer_tan); use strict structured output.
3. Validation: cross-check Form-16 TDS against Form-26AS TDS; flag mismatches for user attention; never auto-correct without consent.
4. PDF handling: text-extractable PDFs via pdfplumber + LLM for tabular reconciliation; scanned PDFs via vision LLM page-by-page.
5. Indic / regional: many rent receipts are in Hindi/Tamil/Marathi — confirm vision model handles these.
6. PII: mask PAN/Aadhaar in logs (last 4 only), encrypt docs at rest, delete after filing + 7-year statutory retention metadata only.
7. Eval: 200 real docs across 10 employer formats; field-level accuracy; INR cost / doc.
8. Cost model: vision-LLM call per page (typical Form-16 = 2 pages = INR ~3), bank statement 30 pages = INR ~40 — design batching to amortise.
9. Confidence + human-in-loop: any field with confidence < 0.9 surfaced to user for confirmation.
10. Audit trail: store extraction prompt-version, model, response per field; required for IT-department queries.
11. DPDP: explicit consent during onboarding, purpose limited to ITR, deletion API.

prompts.title: Agent Eval Harness
prompts.prompt:
Build an eval harness specifically for agentic workflows. Existing eval frameworks score single-turn LLM outputs; I need multi-step task evals.

Agent: {{describe — e.g., books a meeting, processes a refund, drafts and sends an email after approval}}.

Deliver:
1. Scenario schema (yaml): id, persona, initial_state, user_messages (sequence), expected_tool_calls (with args matchers), expected_final_state, must_not_call (list), max_steps, max_INR.
2. Sandbox tools: mock implementations of each real tool that record calls and return canned responses; use a recording proxy to capture real prod calls and replay.
3. Runner: spins up agent against sandbox, walks through user messages, asserts (a) expected tool sequence (allow reorder if independent), (b) final state match, (c) no forbidden calls, (d) within step + INR budget.
4. Soft metrics: trajectory-similarity vs gold (Levenshtein on tool-call sequence), step efficiency, hallucinated tool-call rate.
5. LLM-judge for natural-language outputs in the trajectory (politeness, accuracy of summarisation).
6. Stochastic re-runs: each scenario 3x at temp > 0; compute pass-rate; ship gates require 90% pass.
7. Adversarial scenarios: prompt-injection in tool outputs, ambiguous user requests, mid-task user change-of-mind.
8. CI integration: nightly full suite, per-PR smoke (10 critical scenarios); cost ceiling INR per run.
9. Reporting: dashboard with success-rate by scenario class, regression diff vs last green build.
10. Human review queue for borderline failures.

prompts.title: Caching ROI Memo
prompts.prompt:
Write a 1-page memo to my CTO justifying investment in semantic + prompt caching for our LLM product.

Current state: {{INR/month spend, top-5 features by token volume, dominant model}}.

Deliver:
1. Cache types in scope:
   - Prompt cache (Anthropic / OpenAI server-side, 0.1x cost on cache hits).
   - Semantic cache (GPTCache / Redis-vector — match similar queries, return cached answer).
   - Embedding cache (avoid re-embedding identical chunks).
   - Tool-call cache (deterministic tools).
2. Per-cache hit-rate estimate from traffic analysis (top-1000 queries cover X% of traffic).
3. INR savings model: prompt cache saves Y/month, semantic cache saves Z/month; combined.
4. Implementation effort: prompt-cache = 1 day (just add cache_control), semantic cache = 1 week (Redis + similarity threshold + invalidation), tool-cache = 2 days.
5. Risks: stale answers from semantic cache (invalidate on content updates), cache-poisoning, hit-rate decay if traffic diversifies, INR cost of running Redis cluster.
6. Quality guard: A/B with cache off vs on; measure helpfulness regression; auto-bypass cache for low-confidence matches.
7. Payback period in days; break-even traffic.
8. Recommendation: prioritise prompt cache (highest ROI, lowest risk), defer semantic cache to phase 2.
9. Metrics dashboard: hit rate, INR saved, p95 latency improvement.

prompts.title: Batch Inference Plan
prompts.prompt:
Design a batch-inference workflow for {{task — e.g., re-score all support tickets, regenerate embeddings, classify 10M past records, periodic eval-on-prod}}.

Volume: {{records}}
Deadline: {{hours}}
Budget: INR {{amount}}

Deliver:
1. Provider pick: OpenAI Batch API (50% off, 24h SLA), Anthropic Message Batches (50% off), Together batch endpoints, self-host on Modal A100s (cost-out at INR per A100-hour).
2. INR cost comparison at my volume across the 4 options.
3. Job design: shard inputs by 10k each, parallel submit, store outputs in S3 Mumbai with idempotent keys; dedup retries.
4. Schema: input.jsonl with custom_id per row; output.jsonl reassembled in order.
5. Error handling: per-row retry on transient errors; quarantine permanently-failing rows for human review.
6. Throughput math: with batch API, can ship N records/day; with self-host, gpu-hours = total_tokens / model_throughput.
7. Self-host on Modal: vLLM with paged-attention, batch-size 64, FP8 if supported; auto-scale GPUs.
8. Cost-attribution: log INR per shard for finance.
9. Quality sample: random 1% audited; if error rate > threshold, halt and re-run with fixed prompt.
10. DPDP: PII redaction before sending to any non-India provider; or use only India-hosted Bedrock / Vertex Mumbai.
11. Scheduler: Airflow / Prefect / Modal cron with retry.

prompts.title: Model Tiering Strategy
prompts.prompt:
Design a model-tiering strategy so we use a cheap model where possible and an expensive one only when needed.

Features and current models: {{list}}.
Monthly INR spend: {{amount}}.

Deliver:
1. Tier ladder: T1 = cheapest (Haiku, GPT-4o-mini, Gemini Flash, Llama-3-8B); T2 = mid (Sonnet, GPT-4o, Llama-3-70B); T3 = top (Opus, GPT-4-Turbo, Gemini 1.5 Pro). For each tier list INR/1k tok and quality benchmark.
2. Routing strategies:
   - Static: assign tier per feature (low-stakes summarisation = T1, contract review = T3).
   - Dynamic complexity-router: small classifier (distilbert or T1 LLM) predicts difficulty; route accordingly.
   - Cascade: try T1 first, if confidence/judge-score below threshold, escalate to T2, then T3. Show INR savings model.
   - Speculative: T1 drafts, T2 verifies tokens (provider-supported speculative decoding).
3. Pick the right strategy per feature; usually cascade for QA, static for classification.
4. Confidence signals: log-prob, self-rated confidence, judge score, format-validation pass.
5. Eval: ensure cascaded system matches T3-only quality within 2% on golden set; INR savings projected.
6. Latency trade-off: cascade adds latency on escalation; cap at 1 escalation hop.
7. Multi-provider failover combined with tiering using LiteLLM router.
8. Dashboard: tier mix per feature, INR saved, escalation rate, quality drift.
9. Rollout: shadow mode for 1 week; if quality intact, ship.

prompts.title: Razorpay Billing for LLM Usage
prompts.prompt:
Implement usage-based billing in INR for our LLM SaaS using Razorpay. Customers pay per token / per call / per workflow.

Plans: {{describe — e.g., free 100k tokens/mo, starter INR 999 = 5M tokens, growth INR 4999 = 30M tokens, pay-as-you-go INR 0.0003 per token over plan}}.

Deliver:
1. Data model (Postgres): organisations, plans, subscriptions, usage_events (workspace_id, feature, model, input_tokens, output_tokens, INR_cost_internal, INR_charged, timestamp), invoices, payments.
2. Metering: every LLM call writes a usage_event in the same transaction as the response cache; idempotent on request_id.
3. Aggregation: hourly rollup per workspace per feature into usage_daily; reconcile against provider invoices monthly.
4. Razorpay integration: use Subscriptions API for fixed plans + Invoices API for overage; webhook handlers for payment.captured, subscription.charged, subscription.halted; idempotency on webhook_id.
5. GST: 18% IGST/CGST+SGST per state-of-supply; HSN code 998313; invoice template with GSTIN; reverse-charge for overseas customers.
6. Quotas + soft / hard limits: warn at 80%, soft-throttle at 100%, hard-stop at 120% with grace.
7. Cost margin guard: alert if a customer's LLM cost > 70% of their plan price (negative-margin tenant).
8. India-specific: e-invoicing IRN if turnover > INR 5 Cr, Razorpay auto-collect for autopay (UPI / cards / netbanking), refund flow with audit log.
9. Dashboard: in-app usage meter, downloadable invoices, projected month-end bill.
10. Tests: simulate plan upgrade mid-cycle, downgrade, refund, failed payment retries.

prompts.title: DPDP-Act Compliance for LLM Data Flows
prompts.prompt:
Audit our LLM product for compliance with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP), 2023 and produce a remediation plan.

Product: {{describe}}.
Data touchpoints: {{user inputs, retrieval corpus, fine-tuning data, observability logs, judge calls}}.
Providers: {{list — flag those with India region}}.

Deliver:
1. DPDP applicability map: identify Personal Data and Sensitive Personal Data flowing into prompts, RAG corpora, fine-tuning sets, eval sets, and traces.
2. Lawful basis: confirm consent (with purpose specification) or legitimate use; document the consent UI text.
3. Data principal rights:
   - Access: API to export everything tied to a user.
   - Correction.
   - Erasure: delete from Postgres, vector DB (by metadata filter), object store, observability traces, eval sets, fine-tuning checkpoints (declare if not erasable from a trained model).
   - Grievance: named DPO contact; 30-day SLA.
4. Cross-border transfer: many LLM APIs route to US/EU. Options: use providers with India region (Bedrock Mumbai, Vertex Mumbai, Azure OpenAI India, Sarvam, Together via India proxy, self-host on AWS Mumbai). Document approved cross-border flows.
5. Data minimisation: redact PII (Aadhaar, PAN, phone, email, address) from prompts and from logs using Presidio + Indian-specific recognisers; show code.
6. Retention: define per-data-class retention; auto-purge job.
7. Security: encryption at rest (KMS) and in transit (TLS 1.3), token-level access controls, RBAC, audit log.
8. Notice: privacy notice in English + 22 scheduled languages link.
9. Significant Data Fiduciary triggers: if you cross thresholds, expect DPIA + DPO + audits.
10. Vendor DPA checklist: every LLM / vector / observability vendor needs a signed DPA aligned to DPDP.
11. 90-day remediation Gantt.

prompts.title: Data Residency Architecture
prompts.prompt:
Architect data-residency for our LLM product. Some Indian enterprise customers (BFSI, public sector) require all data and inference to stay within India.

Current stack: {{describe}}.
Customer ask: {{specific clauses, e.g., MeitY empanelled, RBI 2023 Guidelines on cloud, SEBI CSCRF}}.

Deliver:
1. Tiered residency offer:
   - Tier A (default): provider APIs (OpenAI / Anthropic) with global routing — for SMB.
   - Tier B (regional): Bedrock Mumbai / Vertex Mumbai / Azure OpenAI India — Claude / GPT / Gemini in India region.
   - Tier C (sovereign): self-hosted open models (Llama-3-70B, Mistral, Sarvam) on AWS Mumbai or on-prem; INR cost premium of ~3-5x.
2. Map components: app, vector DB, embedding model, LLM, observability, backup, eval pipeline — for each, list the tier-B/C compliant option.
3. Network: VPC, PrivateLink to Bedrock, no egress outside India for Tier C; outbound deny-by-default firewall.
4. Encryption: customer-managed KMS keys (CMK) per enterprise customer.
5. Logging: all logs to S3 Mumbai; mask PII before any cross-region replication.
6. Contractual: DPA, SCCs not needed for India-only; flow-down clauses for sub-processors.
7. INR cost premium passed-through to enterprise customers.
8. Audit pack: SOC2 + ISO27001 + DPDP attestation, on-demand evidence.
9. Deployment isolation: separate AWS account per Tier-C customer for blast-radius.

prompts.title: Hindi / Tamil / Bengali Tokenization Audit
prompts.prompt:
Audit how our LLM stack tokenises Indic text (Hindi, Tamil, Bengali) and quantify the cost / latency penalty.

Features using Indic: {{list}}.
Volume of Indic tokens: {{share of total}}.

Deliver:
1. Token-inflation measurement: for a 1000-character Hindi paragraph, how many tokens does GPT-4o tokenizer (cl100k/o200k), Claude tokenizer, Llama-3 tokenizer, Gemma, Sarvam-1, Mistral produce? Show table.
2. Cost penalty in INR: Indic text often takes 2-4x more tokens than equivalent English; this directly multiplies INR/call. Show concrete monthly INR delta.
3. Latency penalty: more tokens = slower TTFT and total time; show p95 deltas.
4. Quality comparison: pick a 50-Q Hindi eval (e.g., Indic-bench, IndicQA) and run on each model; report accuracy.
5. Recommendation: for Indic-heavy workloads, evaluate Indic-tuned models (Sarvam-1, Krutrim, Llama-3 with Indic LoRA) or models with better Indic tokenisers.
6. Pre-processing tricks: detect language, route Indic to better-tokenizer model; transliterate to Roman only when the downstream task tolerates it.
7. Embedding side: bge-m3 and multilingual-e5 handle Devanagari well; OpenAI embeddings underperform on Indic — measure on your eval.
8. Hinglish / code-mix: most tokenisers struggle; show fallback rule.
9. RAG impact: Indic chunking should respect script boundaries; avoid mid-character splits.
10. INR savings projection if you route Indic traffic to a better-fit model.
11. Decision and rollout.

prompts.title: Prompt Library Versioning + Governance
prompts.prompt:
Set up versioning and governance for our prompt library so multiple teams can safely evolve prompts in production.

Teams: {{n}}, prompts in scope: {{count}}.

Deliver:
1. Storage choice: git-only (yaml in repo), Langfuse Prompts (UI + API + versioning), Braintrust, custom Postgres. Compare DPDP-fit, audit trail, non-engineer access, rollback ergonomics.
2. Schema per prompt: id, semver, model_compat, input_vars (typed), system_prompt, examples, temperature, max_tokens, owner, last_eval_score, last_changed_at, tags.
3. Branching workflow: feature branch -> PR -> CI runs eval suite -> reviewer approves -> merge -> auto-deploy to staging -> canary 10% -> full.
4. Per-environment pinning: prod points to v1.4.2; staging points to latest; never auto-bump prod.
5. Rollback: one-click revert to previous version pointer; log who/when/why.
6. Access control: PMs can edit non-safety prompts via Langfuse UI; safety / compliance prompts require security-team approval.
7. Eval gating: cannot promote a prompt without passing the regression suite.
8. Change-log generated automatically per release.
9. Observability: every inference logs prompt_id + version_hash; dashboards filter by version.
10. A/B framework: traffic split on version, primary + guardrail metrics, auto-stop on guardrail breach.
11. Onboarding doc: 1-pager 'how to add a new prompt' and 'how to roll back'.
12. Quarterly audit: stale prompts flagged for review.

prompts.title: Streaming Tool-Use with Mid-Stream Decisions
prompts.prompt:
I need to support streaming responses where the LLM may decide mid-stream to call a tool, then resume. Provider: {{Anthropic / OpenAI}}.

Use case: {{e.g., chat where the agent fetches data when the user asks a factual question}}.

Deliver:
1. Provider-specific behaviour: how Anthropic streams tool_use blocks (content_block_start / delta / stop with input JSON), how OpenAI streams tool_calls in chunks.
2. Backend handler (Python / Node) that:
   - parses streaming events,
   - detects tool_use block,
   - pauses forwarding to client (or forwards a 'tool-running' UI event),
   - executes tool (with timeout + retry),
   - sends tool_result back to LLM and streams the continuation,
   - resumes forwarding text tokens to client.
3. SSE event types: text_delta, tool_call_started, tool_call_completed, error, done.
4. Multi-tool per turn: handle parallel tool_use blocks (Claude can emit multiple).
5. UI affordance: show 'Looking up your order...' card while tool runs; replace with answer when stream resumes.
6. Latency: tool latency adds to total; show p95 budget; consider speculative pre-fetch for common tools.
7. Cancellation: client disconnect -> abort upstream + abort tool.
8. Cost: token accounting must include tool_result tokens passed back to LLM.
9. Eval: 30 scenarios with required tool sequence; assert correctness of mid-stream control flow.
10. Failure handling: tool 5xx -> retry once -> return graceful 'I could not fetch that' to model.

prompts.title: Long-Context vs RAG Decision
prompts.prompt:
Should we stuff the full corpus into Claude/Gemini's long context (200k-2M tokens) or stick with RAG?

Corpus: {{tokens}}
Query rate: {{QPS}}
Latency SLO: {{ms}}
Budget: INR {{amount}}/month.

Deliver:
1. INR cost comparison at my volume:
   - Long-context: every query pays full corpus tokens (mitigated by prompt cache 0.1x).
   - RAG: pays embedding (one-time) + retrieval LLM tokens (small) + answer LLM tokens (small).
   Show monthly INR for both.
2. Latency: long-context has higher TTFT (model must process all tokens unless cached); RAG has retrieval overhead (~50-200ms).
3. Quality: RAG can suffer recall miss; long-context can suffer 'lost-in-the-middle'. Score on my golden set.
4. Update cadence: long-context with prompt-cache works only if corpus is mostly stable (cache eviction on every change); RAG handles streaming updates better.
5. Hybrid: RAG to retrieve top-50 chunks, then put all 50 in long context for the LLM to reason across — best of both.
6. Decision rule: if corpus < 200k tokens AND fits in cache AND updates rarely -> long-context with cache. Otherwise RAG.
7. Multi-document reasoning: long-context wins for cross-doc synthesis; RAG wins for high-precision lookup.
8. POC plan: implement both, run eval, pick winner per feature.
9. Compliance: long-context with cached PII has the same DPDP exposure as RAG.

prompts.title: Synthetic Data Generation Pipeline
prompts.prompt:
Generate a synthetic dataset for {{purpose: fine-tuning, eval, augmenting under-represented intents, Hindi data scarcity}}.

Target: {{N examples, format}}
Quality bar: {{describe}}
Budget: INR {{amount}}.

Deliver:
1. Generator pick: Claude Sonnet (best quality, INR ~0.5/example), GPT-4o, Llama-3-70B (cheaper), Gemini Flash (cheapest). Decide based on quality bar.
2. Seed prompt design: provide 5-10 hand-written gold examples + clear instructions on style, format, edge-case coverage.
3. Diversity strategies:
   - vary persona, language (Hindi/Tamil/Bengali/English/Hinglish), difficulty, length, edge-cases;
   - sample seeds from different topics;
   - use temperature 0.8-1.0 + top-p sampling;
   - dedup with MinHash to drop near-duplicates.
4. Verifier loop: a second LLM (different model to avoid same-model bias) scores each generated example; keep only > 4/5.
5. Human spot-check: 5% sample reviewed by annotators; if reject-rate > 10%, fix the generator prompt.
6. License + provenance: log generator model, prompt version, seed source; document in dataset card.
7. DPDP: generated data may inadvertently mimic real PII; run PII redaction pass.
8. Anti-leak: keep synthetic out of eval set if used for training.
9. INR cost projection: N examples * tokens_per_example * model_rate.
10. Iteration plan: generate -> verify -> sample-review -> fix prompt -> regenerate.
11. Storage: versioned in S3 Mumbai with hash.

prompts.title: vLLM Deployment Plan on Modal
prompts.prompt:
Deploy {{Llama-3-70B / Mistral-Large / Qwen2-72B}} on Modal with vLLM. Production traffic: {{QPS}}, p95 latency target: {{ms}}.

Deliver:
1. GPU pick: A100 80GB x N, H100 x N, A10 x N — based on model size, KV-cache, and concurrency. Show calc and INR/hr at Modal rates.
2. Quantisation: FP16 vs FP8 vs AWQ-INT4 vs GPTQ; trade-off quality vs throughput vs memory. Recommend with eval delta.
3. vLLM config: tensor_parallel_size, max_model_len, gpu_memory_utilization, max_num_seqs, enforce_eager, enable_prefix_caching, speculative decoding (draft model).
4. Autoscaling: Modal cold-start mitigation (keep_warm), scale-up policy on queue depth, scale-to-zero off-hours.
5. INR cost model: GPU-hours * rate vs Together / Bedrock / Replicate per 1M tokens. Break-even at X tokens/month.
6. Throughput benchmark: vllm bench / vllm serve + locust at expected prompt + completion lengths; report tokens/s and concurrent requests at SLO.
7. Multi-LoRA: serve N customer fine-tunes from one base model with vLLM LoRA adapters; cost-attribution per tenant.
8. Observability: Prometheus metrics from vLLM, Grafana dashboard (queue depth, KV-cache util, batch size, p95 latency).
9. Health checks + canary: 10% traffic shift, watch metrics, rollback rule.
10. India residency: Modal regions; if Mumbai unavailable, document the residency caveat to enterprise customers.
11. Failure modes: OOM, GPU eviction, request timeout, fallback to API provider.

prompts.title: Hugging Face Model Selection Workflow
prompts.prompt:
I am picking an open model from Hugging Face for {{task}}. Walk me through a rigorous selection workflow.

Constraints: {{licence (commercial ok?), parameter ceiling, language coverage, latency SLO, budget INR/month}}.

Deliver:
1. Filter by licence (Apache-2 / MIT / Llama-Community / Gemma) — exclude non-commercial if needed.
2. Shortlist via leaderboards relevant to my task: Open LLM Leaderboard, MTEB (embeddings), BigCode (code), MIRACL (multilingual retrieval), Indic-LLM-leaderboard.
3. Pick top 5; for each: parameters, context length, training data summary, base vs instruct variant, known biases.
4. Quick local eval: spin up on Modal A10, run my 50-question golden set, report accuracy + latency + tokens/s.
5. Compare to closed-API baseline (Claude Haiku / GPT-4o-mini); only pick open if (cost AND quality) win or compliance demands it.
6. Fine-tunability: does the model accept LoRA cleanly, has community produced strong domain fine-tunes I can build on?
7. Vendor risk: model card, maintainer, security incidents.
8. Deployment plan: vLLM / TGI / llama.cpp / Ollama based on infra.
9. INR TCO: GPU-hours, ops, eval, fine-tune budget vs API pass-through.
10. Final pick + 1 risk + decision-revisit trigger.

prompts.title: Function-Calling Schema Design
prompts.prompt:
Design function-calling / tool schemas for our agent. Tools: {{list with intended behaviour}}.

Deliver:
1. Schema audit checklist applied per tool:
   - Name: verb_noun, < 25 chars, snake_case.
   - Description: 1-2 lines explaining when to use AND when NOT to use.
   - Parameters: typed (string/int/enum/array/object), required vs optional, examples in description, max-length on free-text, enums closed.
   - Pre-conditions: state required before call (e.g., user_authenticated).
   - Post-conditions: side-effects clearly stated.
   - Idempotency: include idempotency_key when side-effecting.
2. Anthropic vs OpenAI schema syntax differences; show side-by-side example.
3. Strict mode: OpenAI strict: true, Anthropic disable_parallel_tool_use when needed; trade-offs.
4. Response schema: structured JSON tool result; never return free-text from a tool when avoidable.
5. Error contract: tool returns {ok: bool, error_code, message}; the model is instructed how to handle each error.
6. Naming hygiene: avoid synonym tools (search vs lookup vs find) — model gets confused.
7. Tool-count budget: > 20 tools degrade selection quality; route via a meta-tool or split agents.
8. Eval: 30 prompts, assert (a) right tool picked, (b) args validate, (c) refusal when no tool fits.
9. Doc generation: produce markdown docs from schemas for human reviewers.
10. Versioning: bump tool schema with semver; backward-compat for in-flight agents.

prompts.title: Embedding Drift Monitoring
prompts.prompt:
Set up monitoring for embedding drift in our RAG system. We retrain / swap embedding models periodically and want to catch silent quality regressions.

Vector DB: {{Qdrant / Pinecone / pgvector}}. Volume: {{vectors}}.

Deliver:
1. Drift signals to track:
   - Query-distribution shift: KL divergence of query-embedding distribution week over week.
   - Retrieval-quality drop: recall@5 on a fixed 200-question golden set, run nightly.
   - Score distribution: median similarity score per query type — sudden drop = something changed.
   - Out-of-distribution rate: queries whose nearest-neighbour score < threshold.
   - Re-ranker disagreement: cases where bi-encoder top-1 != cross-encoder top-1 (rate trend).
2. Pipeline: nightly job pulls 10k random production queries, computes embeddings, runs ANN, scores against golden when applicable, writes metrics to Prometheus / BigQuery.
3. Alerts: recall@5 drop > 5%, OOD rate > 15%, score-distribution shift z-score > 3.
4. Embedding-model A/B: when evaluating a new embedding model, dual-index for 1 week, run both nightly, compare; only cut-over on win.
5. Re-embed cost in INR if model swap is approved.
6. Stale-vector detection: vectors > 90 days old in a fast-moving corpus get re-embedded.
7. Tenant-level drift: per-tenant recall to catch tenant-specific regressions.
8. Dashboard in Grafana / Metabase.
9. Runbook for the on-call when an alert fires.

prompts.title: Production LLM Postmortem Template
prompts.prompt:
We had a production LLM incident: {{describe — e.g., model started returning blank responses, hallucination spike, INR cost 10x overnight, agent stuck in loop, vendor outage}}.

Write a blameless postmortem.

Deliver:
1. Header: incident ID, severity (S0-S3), date, duration, customer impact (% requests, INR refunded, SLA breach), authors.
2. Timeline (UTC + IST): detection, escalation, mitigation, resolution; minute-resolution.
3. Root cause: deepest technical reason; differentiate trigger from contributing factors. Examples:
   - prompt change deployed without eval gate,
   - vendor model silently rolled to a new snapshot,
   - rate-limit handler missing exponential backoff caused thundering herd,
   - retrieved chunk contained prompt-injection that hijacked the agent,
   - cache key collision returned wrong tenant's answer (DPDP escalation).
4. Why it was not caught: gaps in eval coverage, missing alert, no canary, no kill-switch.
5. Customer comms: who was told, what was said, what was offered (credit in INR).
6. Action items: each with owner, JIRA, deadline, severity. Categories: prevent (eval gate, schema validation), detect (alert, dashboard), mitigate (kill-switch, fallback model), respond (runbook, on-call drill).
7. Risk-reduction estimate: how each action item reduces probability or impact.
8. DPDP / regulatory: if PII leaked, notice timeline (72h to Data Protection Board if material).
9. Lessons: 3 lessons phrased for the org, not the individual.
10. Follow-up review date.
11. Append: full chat transcripts, prompt versions, model versions, traces.
